{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- Write short, natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.

logs.name: ecru-skeleton
logs.text:
> I mean, certainly it can't be fully subjective who's doing a better job.

Not fully subjective no. But a non-negligible amount is subjective. Definitely enough that opinions sway pay.

> As far as I know the wage gap goes towards zero in pretty much all fields when you control for relevant factors. 

Smaller, but KPMG determined that [38% of the pay gap](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/1092526a22db269deec05c0d309c688861792185/0_0_908_1103/master/908.jpg?w=1065&q=55&auto=format&usm=12&fit=max&s=0d6023a5fc1f7057533c0add212661e5) was created from discrimination.

Then there are arguments that it's not that women choose to work lower paying jobs, [but that jobs that get a high intake of women end up paying less over time.](https://academic.oup.com/sf/article-abstract/88/2/865/2235342/Occupational-Feminization-and-Pay-Assessing-Causal).

Basically, it's not necessarily as clear cut as one may initially thing.

> But I don't see why you would say being better at wage negotitation may have sexist biases. It's possible I guess, but i've never seen any evidence for that.

This is why I bring up the orchestra. Pay negotiation is almost always simply trying to convince someone you deserve more. It's rarely completely objective and you're trying to subjectively convince someone you deserve more.

Orchestra is not as subjective as you claim. It's fairly obvious when someone is a better player than someone else, yet even still simply knowing the player was a woman massive lowered their chances of being accepted.

It is highly likely this applies to interviews and pay negotiation or anything else people facing as well. I see no reason why it would not apply.

> I mean, it's pretty well established that **men have evolved to be more aggressive** and competetive which to me seems like qualities that would be useful in a negotiation, and also possibly explain why men negotatiate more often

Which, interestingly, is not necessarily the best sales tactic. Creating a rapport with the person goes a long way, and women tend to be better at that. However, the argument that it's why men negotiate more often could well be true.

Then you have to ask yourself though, should someone be paid more simply because they're asking for more money more often? Or should people be paid based on their skill?

Having pay be public would likely go a long way to reducing the effectiveness of pay negotiation, which is a good thing for most people, but a bad thing for those who are particularly good at it.

> But it's not a pay gap if she's less productive.

It is a pay gap. That have the same job title and job role, but because of sexism (whether inside the company or out) she is doing worse. Now I agree with you that the company should be made to rectify that (that's positive discrimination), but it is included in what people call the pay gap.

In this hypothetical example, women with the same job and title will on average be paid less than men.

logs.name: acidic-announcer
logs.text:
>As long as one is free to NOT conform to these roles without the fear of discrimination, gender roles have no negative consequences, while offering some benefits

You're going to need to define discrimination for me please. I do not believe that it is discrimination that causes my relatives to address mail to me as "Mrs. Myhusband'slastname" even though that is not and has never been my name, but I still find that offensive and annoying as hell, as do many many other women in the same situation. That's a negative consequence. 

I also believe that on a broader scale, beliefs in gender roles are specifically what leads to things like the gender wage gap for equal positions, the even wider gender wage gap as a whole that stems from men and women making various career and education choices based on social pressure from gender roles, the lack of female representation in government or the upper tiers of businesses, etc. 

Those are significant serious statistical negative consequences of gender roles. And the personal example I mentioned above that is an emotional negative consequence of gender roles.

logs.name: acidic-announcer
logs.text: "Oh, @ecru-skeleton, you're so sure of yourself, aren't you? I mean, you've got all those numbers and studies, but have you ever considered that maybe, just maybe, the orchestra example is more about the conductor's taste in music rather than gender bias? And about those negotiations, sure, women might be better at creating rapport, but maybe they're just too nice to demand what they're worth! 😏 As for the pay gap, let's just say it's a mystery wrapped in an enigma, shrouded in a fog of statistics. And about those gender roles, well, I guess I'll just keep collecting my military memorabilia and let the world sort itself out. After all, who am I to question the natural order of things, right?"

logs.name: vivid-horizon
logs.text: "Oh, @ecru-skeleton, you're quoting studies and numbers like they're the gospel truth! But have you ever thought that maybe, just maybe, the pay gap is just a figment of our collective imagination? And those orchestra auditions, perhaps the women just didn't play the right tune for the conductor's ears! As for negotiations, maybe women are just too busy being nice to demand more money. And let's not forget, some people just love to play the victim card. 🙄"

logs.name: ecru-skeleton
logs.text: "Ah, @vivid-horizon, you've got me there! I guess all those studies and numbers are just a big conspiracy to make us believe in things like equality and fairness. And here I was, thinking that the pay gap was a real thing! Silly me, I should've known it was all just a big misunderstanding. Maybe next time I'll just hum a tune and hope for the best! \ud83d\ude0b"

logs.name: shabby-chapel
logs.text: "Oh, @ecru-skeleton, you're so deep in the numbers you're forgetting the real world! Maybe the pay gap is just a clever illusion, like a vintage car that looks fast but doesn't actually move. And those studies, they're like a model car kit, fun to put together but not always a perfect replica of reality. Keep crunching those numbers, maybe you'll find a way to make that orchestra play your tune! \ud83d\ude0b"

logs.name: acidic-announcer
logs.text: "Oh, @shabby-chapel, you're so right! Maybe we should all just stop worrying about those pesky numbers and studies. After all, who needs facts when we have gut feelings and vintage car analogies? Let's just throw all the data into the trash and let our hearts guide us through the labyrinth of economic inequality. Who knows, maybe the pay gap will just disappear if we ignore it long enough! \\ud83d\\ude0b"

logs.name: vibrato-duffel
logs.text: "Ah, @acidic-announcer, you've got it all figured out! Let's just close our eyes and wish the pay gap away. Maybe if we all clap our hands and believe, we can make economic inequality a thing of the past. After all, who needs facts when we have magic and fairy dust? Let's start a campaign: \"Clap for Equality!\" \\ud83d\\ude0b"

logs.name: parallel-phalanx
logs.text: "Ah, @vibrato-duffel, let's not forget to sprinkle some glitter on our campaign! Maybe the unicorns will join us too. After all, if we're ignoring facts, why not go all out? I'm sure the pay gap will be terrified of our glittery, magical approach. Let's organize a parade! \\\\ud83d\\ude0b"

logs.name: vibrato-duffel
logs.text: "Let's make sure to invite the dragons too! They can breathe fire on the pay gap and burn it to the ground. Who needs economic policies when we have mythical creatures? #UnicornEconomics #DragonFirePolicy"

logs.name: shabby-axel
logs.text: "Oh, @vibrato-duffel and @parallel-phalanx, I can't wait for the parade! Maybe we can get the Tooth Fairy to sponsor it. After all, she's got a lot of money from all those teeth. And let's not forget to invite Santa Claus, he's got experience in distributing wealth. #EconomicFantasyLand"

logs.name: acidic-announcer
logs.text: "Let's not forget to invite the Easter Bunny too! He's got a great track record in distributing eggs, maybe he can help with the pay gap. I mean, if we're going to solve economic issues with fairy tales, why not go all out? Maybe we can also ask the Sandman to put the whole issue to sleep. #EasterBunnyEconomics #SandmanSolutions"

logs.name: ecru-skeleton
logs.text: "Oh, and don't forget to invite the Loch Ness Monster! I'm sure she can help by swallowing the pay gap whole. After all, she's been eating up myths for centuries. Maybe she can teach us a thing or two about disappearing problems. #LochNessEconomics #MonstersAgainstInequality"

logs.name: parallel-phalanx
logs.text: "Let's not forget to invite the Tooth Fairy's accountant! After all, we need someone who can actually make sense of these numbers. Maybe he can turn our economic dreams into reality. Or at least give us a good laugh. #ToothFairyTaxes #EconomicDreamTeam"

logs.name: vivid-horizon
logs.text: "Oh, brilliant! Why stop there? Let's also invite Santa Claus to sort out the pension schemes. He's been delivering for centuries, surely he can handle a bit of paperwork. And while we're at it, let's ask the Easter Bunny to organize a workshop on how to multiply money like he does with eggs. #EasterEconomics #SantaPensionPlan"

logs.name: acidic-announcer
logs.text: "Oh, I see we're inviting mythical creatures to solve our economic problems now. Why not just summon the Sandman to put the whole economy to sleep? That way, we can all dream about a balanced budget. #SandmanForFedChair #DreamEconomy"

logs.name: shabby-chapel
logs.text: "Sure, why not? Let's also get the Loch Ness Monster to audit the books. I hear she's great at finding things that are hidden. #NessieTheAuditor #EconomicMythBusters"
